Q 9

There is a marbles competition in your neighbourhood with the team in the next street. You want to buy new marbles for the competition. You have 150 saved up. You go to a shop to buy the marbles. What are the qualities you would look for in the marbles so that you can win the competition?

Solution

To win the competition, I would look for marbles that are:

  1. Perfectly round so they roll smoothly and accurately.
  2. Durable and strong they should not break easily during the game.
  3. Well-balanced in weight neither too light nor too heavy for easy control.
  4. Smooth and polished surface for better aim and glide.
  5. Brightly coloured or transparent so I can easily spot them in the game. I would also compare prices at different shops to get the best quality within my ₹ 150 budget.

sections.label: Questions and activities
sections.html:
Q 1

What are the main features of a market? Recall a recent visit to a market to purchase a product. What are the different features of a market that you observed during this visit?

Solution

A market is a place where buyers and sellers interact to exchange goods and services. The main features of a market include the presence of buyers and sellers, price determination through demand and supply, availability of multiple products, and competition among sellers. During a recent visit to a vegetable market, I observed that there were many stalls selling the same type of vegetables at different prices. Some vendors allowed bargaining, while others had fixed prices. The market was crowded, and fresh produce was being restocked continuously.

Q 2

Remember the epigraph from a famous economist at the beginning of the chapter? Discuss its relevance in the context of the chapter you have read.

Solution

The epigraph at the beginning of the chapter provides insight into how markets function and influence economic activities. It is relevant because it highlights key aspects such as demand and supply, pricing, competition, and the role of buyers and sellers. Understanding the quote helps in relating economic theories to real-life market situations.

Q 3

In the example of buying and selling of guavas, imagine that the seller is getting a good price, and is able to make a profit. He will try to get more guavas from farmers to be able to sell them at the same price and increase his earnings. What is the farmer likely to do in this kind of a situation? Do you think he will start thinking about the demand for guavas in the next season? What is likely to be his response?

Solution

If the seller gets a good price for guavas, he will demand more from farmers. The farmer, noticing the high demand, may try to produce more guavas in the next season to increase his income. He might also consider expanding his farmland, using better farming techniques, or storing guavas to sell when prices rise. However, he will also assess market trends to avoid overproduction, which could lead to lower prices.

Q 4

Match the following types of markets with their characteristics:

Markets Criteria
Physical market Goods and services flow outside the nation's boundaries
Online market Deals in bulk quantities
Domestic market Serves the final consumers with goods and services
International market Requires physical presence of buyers and sellers
Wholesale market Buyers and sellers meet virtually and can transact at any time
Retail market Lies within the boundaries of a nation
Solution
Markets Criteria
Physical market Requires physical presence of buyers and sellers
Online market Buyers and sellers meet virtually and can transact at any time
Domestic market Lies within the boundaries of a nation
International market Goods and services flow outside the nation's boundaries
Wholesale market Deals in bulk quantities
Retail market Serves the final consumers with goods and services
Q 5

Prices are generally determined by the interaction between demand from buyers and supply by sellers. Can you think of products where prices are high despite lesser number of buyers demanding the product? What could be the reasons for that?

Solution

Some products remain expensive despite having fewer buyers due to factors like exclusivity, branding, and production costs. Examples include luxury cars, designer clothing, rare antiques, and limited-edition electronics. These products are priced high due to their uniqueness, high-quality materials, and brand value, making them desirable to a select group of buyers.

Q 6

Look at the real life situation that a retail seller of vegetables encountered: A family came to shop for vegetables. The price of beans that the seller on the cart was offering was? ₹ 30/kg. The lady started to bargain with the seller to bring the price down to ₹ 25/kg. The seller protested and refused to sell at that price saying he would make a loss at that price. The lady walks away. The family then goes to a super bazaar nearby. They buy vegetables in the super bazaar where they pay ₹ 40/kg for the beans that is neatly packed in a plastic bag. What are the reasons that the family does this? Are there factors that affect buying and selling which are not directly connected to price?

Solution

The family may have bought vegetables at a higher price from the super bazaar due to convenience, cleanliness, better packaging, or trust in the store’s quality. Factors like hygiene, availability of multiple products in one place, and a preference for organized retail stores also influence buying decisions. Sometimes, customers are willing to pay more for perceived quality and shopping experience rather than just focusing on the price.

Q 7

There are some districts in India that are famous for growing tomatoes. However, during some seasons, the situation is not good for farmers. With a large quantity of harvest, there are reports of farmers throwing away their produce and all their hard work going to waste. Why do you think farmers do this? What role can wholesalers play in such situations? What are the possible ways of ensuring that the tomatoes are not wasted, and the farmers are also not at a loss?

Solution

Farmers throw away tomatoes during a surplus season because excessive supply leads to low prices, making it unprofitable to sell them. Wholesalers can help by buying in bulk at a reasonable price and distributing them to different regions. Possible solutions to prevent wastage include setting up food processing industries to make tomato-based products, government support in price regulation, better storage facilities, and linking farmers to alternative markets to ensure they get fair prices.

Q 8

Have you heard about or visited a school carnival / fair organized by your school or any other school? Discuss with your friends and teachers about the kind of activities organized by students there. How do they conduct selling and negotiation with the buyers?

Solution

Do it your shelf.

Q 9

Choose any 5 products and check out the label with the certification signs discussed in the chapter. Did you find products that did not have a logo? Why do you think this is so?

Solution

Do it your shelf.

Q 10

You and your classmates have manufactured a soap bar. Design a label for its packaging. What in your opinion should be mentioned on the label for the consumer to know the product better?

Solution

Do it yourself.

sections.label: The Big Questions
sections.html:
Q 1

What are the main characteristics of Indian agriculture?

Solution

The main Characteristics of Indian Agriculture are:

  1. Deep Cultural Roots: The word agriculture comes from Latin words ‘agri’ means ‘field’ and ‘culture’ means ‘to cultivate’. Indian agriculture is not limited to growing crops; it includes livestock rearing, beekeeping (apiculture), fisheries, silkworm rearing, silk production, and fibre production like cotton and hemp.
  2. Strong Economic Importance: Agriculture and allied activities contribute more than 18% to India’s GDP (2022-23). Nearly 46% of India’s working population is engaged in farming and related activities, making it a major source of livelihood and national development.
  3. Traditional Family Connection: Most Indian families have been connected to farming for generations. This long association shows the deep cultural, emotional, and economic bond between people and the land.
  4. Diverse Crops Across Regions: India has a wide range of climates and soils. This allows farmers to grow many crops-like rice in wetter regions, wheat in cooler areas, and cotton, sugarcane, and millets in warmer places.
  5. Different Farming Practices in Different Areas: Because soils, temperatures, and rainfall vary across India, each region follows unique farming practices and grows crops suited to local conditions. This makes Indian agriculture highly adaptable.
  6. Labour-Rich Agriculture: Farming involves many activities like sowing, weeding, and harvesting. These tasks give work to many people and strengthen rural communities.
  7. Balanced Use of Traditional and Modern Methods: Indian farmers use both traditional tools and modern machines. This combination helps maintain cultural practices while also improving productivity.
Q 2

How are farming, climate, soil, and water interrelated?

Solution

Farming in any region depends mainly on its climate, which decides the temperature and rainfall a place receives. Different climates support different kinds of crops. For example, rice grows well in warm areas with plenty of rain, while wheat grows best in cooler places with moderate rainfall. Some crops like millets and pulses thrive even in dry climates, whereas tea and coffee grow beautifully in hilly regions with cool weather. Thus, the climate of a place guides farmers in choosing the right crops.
Soil also plays a very important role in farming because it provides plants with nutrients. Different soils grow different crops. For instance, alluvial soil is very fertile and supports rice, wheat, sugarcane, and jute. Black soil holds moisture and is ideal for cotton, while red soil is good for groundnut, millets, and pulses. When the soil is rich and healthy, crops grow better and give higher yields. Wherever the soil needs support, farmers improve it using compost and fertilisers.
Water availability further influences farming practices. Crops need the right amount of water to grow, and farmers use rivers, canals, tube wells, and tanks to irrigate their fields where rainfall is not enough. Some crops like sugarcane and paddy need more water, whereas others like bajra or ragi require very little. With the right balance of climate, soil, and water, farmers are able to grow suitable crops and ensure healthy, productive agriculture.
In conclusion, climate, soil, and water work together to decide what kind of farming is possible in any region. The combination of these three factors helps farmers choose the right crops and farming methods, ensuring better productivity and sustainable agriculture.

Q 3

How can traditional practices and contemporary ones complement each other?

Solution

Traditional practices and contemporary ones complement each other in the following ways:
Traditional practices use natural methods like mixed farming, organic manure, and crop rotation. While, contemporary (modern) practices use machines, HYV (High Yielding Variety) seeds, fertilisers, and latest irrigation technology. When both are used together, farming becomes more efficient and ecofriendly. Traditional methods protect the soil and environment, while modern methods increase production. By combining both, farmers can grow healthier crops in a faster and safer way.

sections.label: THINK ABOUT IT
sections.html:
Q 1

When you read or hear the word 'farmer', what do you visualise? Many people imagine a man. However, in rural areas of India, more than 75 per cent of people working in the agriculture sector are women (2025). They perform most of the farming operations, from sowing to harvesting and threshing.

Solution

When you read or hear the word 'farmer', what do you visualise? Many people imagine a man. However, in rural areas of India, more than 75 per cent of people working in the agriculture sector are women (2025). They perform most of the farming operations, from sowing to harvesting and threshing.

Q 2

Imagine a network of small pipes with tiny holes placed near the base of your plants. These pipes are connected to a water source, and when the water is turned on, it drips slowly and steadily from the holes, watering only the roots. This targeted delivery ensures that water is used efficiently, minimising waste.

Solution

The system described is called drip irrigation, a modern and efficient method of watering plants. In this system, small pipes with tiny holes are placed near the base of each plant. These pipes are connected to a water source, and when water flows through them, it drips slowly and directly to the roots of the plants. This targeted delivery ensures that water reaches exactly where it is needed, reducing wastage caused by evaporation or runoff. Drip irrigation is especially useful in areas with limited water supply, as it uses water much more efficiently than traditional methods like flood or sprinkler irrigation. It also helps maintain soil moisture consistently, promotes healthier plant growth, and can improve crop yields. Farmers can use drip irrigation for fields, orchards, or even gardens, making it a sustainable practice that conserves water while supporting agriculture in water-scarce regions.

Q 3

With its rich alluvial soil, for millennia the Ganga basin has been a source of food and water to millions of people - over 500 million today. Yet, for some years, the Ganga river has been under stress and is increasingly unable to replenish itself; parts of it are no longer navigable in summer as the water level is too low even for river boats.
The causes are multiple: global warming is causing Himalayan glaciers to melt at an unprecedented rate; much water is diverted to irrigation; agriculture and industry pump out huge amounts of groundwater; and hundreds of dams interfere with the river's natural flow. Solutions exist, but if this condition of Ganga persists, agriculture in its basin may become less and less sustainable, threatening the livelihood of millions and India's food production.

Solution

The Ganga river, with its fertile alluvial soil, has been a lifeline for millions of people living in its basin for thousands of years. Today, over 500 million people depend on it for food, water, and agriculture. The river supports extensive farming, providing irrigation to vast areas of wheat, rice, sugarcane, and other crops. Its alluvial plains are highly fertile, making the basin one of India’s most productive agricultural regions. However, in recent years, the Ganga has come under severe stress and is increasingly unable to sustain itself. During summer, water levels drop so much that parts of the river become unnavigable, affecting transportation and local livelihoods.
Several factors contribute to this crisis. Global warming is accelerating the melting of Himalayan glaciers, which feed the river, altering its flow patterns. Large amounts of water are diverted for irrigation, while agriculture and industry extract huge volumes of groundwater, reducing the river’s natural replenishment. Additionally, the construction of hundreds of dams and barrages disrupts the natural flow, affecting aquatic life and sediment deposition that maintains soil fertility.
If this situation continues, agriculture in the Ganga basin may become less sustainable. Crop yields could fall due to water scarcity, soil fertility may decline, and farmers’ livelihoods could be threatened. India’s food production may also face challenges, given the region’s importance in national agriculture. Solutions like efficient water management, reduced groundwater extraction, better irrigation practices, and restoration of the river’s natural flow are necessary. Protecting the Ganga is essential not only for the environment but also for sustaining agriculture, supporting livelihoods, and ensuring food security for millions.

sections.label: Questions and activities
sections.html:
Q 1

Why do farmers in Kerala grow rice while farmers in Punjab grow mostly wheat? What would happen if they swapped?

Solution

Farmers in Kerala grow rice because its warm, humid climate and high rainfall suit paddy cultivation. Punjab’s cooler winters and fertile alluvial soil favor wheat. If they swapped, crops would struggle: rice in Punjab would face water scarcity, and wheat in Kerala would suffer from heat, humidity, and disease.

Q 2

Match the following:

Column A Column B
(a) Kharif crops (i) Crops during the winter
(b) Rabi crops (ii) Crops grown during the monsoon
(c) Alluvial soil (iii) Thin, rough, and rocky soil found in mountainous regions
(d) Terrace farming (iv) Crops grown in summer
(e) Alpine soil (v) Soil rich in nutrients deposited by rivers
(f) Zaid crops (vi) Method of farming on hillsides
Solution
Column A Column B
(a) Kharif crops (ii) Crops grown during the monsoon
(b) Rabi crops (i) Crops during the winter
(c) Alluvial soil (v) Soil rich in nutrients deposited by rivers
(d) Terrace farming (vi) Method of farming on hillsides
(e) Alpine soil (iii) Thin, rough, and rocky soil found in mountainous regions
(f) Zaid crops (iv) Crops grown in summer
Q 3

Why do certain crops thrive in specific regions?

Solution

Crops thrive in specific regions because of a combination of environmental and biological factors, mainly:

  1. Climate - Temperature, rainfall, and sunlight affect growth. For example, rice needs warm, wet conditions, while wheat prefers cooler, drier climates.
  2. Soil Type - Nutrient content, pH, texture, and drainage influence which crops grow well. Potatoes prefer well-drained, loamy soil, while cotton grows in sandy soil.
  3. Water Availability - Some crops need a lot of water (e.g., sugarcane), others can survive with less (e.g., millet).
  4. Altitude and Topography - Mountainous or hilly areas have cooler temperatures and different sunlight exposure, affecting crops like tea or coffee.
  5. Pests and Diseases - Regions free from certain pests allow crops to thrive, while others may struggle if vulnerable.
  6. Human Practices - Irrigation, fertilization, and crop rotation can expand the regions where a crop can grow, but natural conditions still limit optimal growth.
Q 4

How has modern technology helped farmers?

Solution

Modern technology aids farmers through precision farming, drones, and sensors to monitor crops, automated machinery for planting and harvesting, improved irrigation systems, and data-driven decision tools. These innovations increase yield, reduce labor, conserve resources, control pests, and help farmers adapt to climate change efficiently and sustainably.

Q 5

Why is sustainable agriculture important? Write a short note on this.

Solution

Sustainable agriculture is important because it meets current food needs without harming the environment or depleting resources for future generations. It conserves soil, water, and biodiversity, reduces pollution, and promotes healthy ecosystems. Sustainable practices ensure long-term food security, protect farmers’ livelihoods, and help combat climate change.

Q 6

Name some challenges that farmers face today. What might be their impact for people?

Solution

Challenges farmers face today:

  1. Climate change - unpredictable weather, droughts, floods.
  2. Pests and diseases - crop loss and reduced yield.
  3. Soil degradation - lower fertility and productivity.
  4. Water scarcity - affects irrigation and crop growth.
  5. Market fluctuations - unstable prices for crops.
  6. High input costs - seeds, fertilizers, machinery.

Impact on people:

  1. Food shortages and higher prices.
  2. Lower quality or less variety of food.
  3. Economic stress on farming communities.
  4. Increased risk of malnutrition and hunger.
Q 7

Have a debate in class on the topic “Traditional irrigation methods are better than modern ones.”

Solution
  1. Opening Statement (Pro-Traditional):
    • Traditional methods like wells, canals, and rainwater harvesting are simple, low-cost, and environmentally friendly.
    • They use local resources and require less energy.
    • Support sustainable farming and preserve water naturally.
  2. Arguments (Pro-Traditional):
    • No dependence on electricity or expensive machinery.
    • Maintains local ecosystems and soil health.
    • Time-tested methods suited to local climate.
  3. Counterpoints (Against Traditional / Pro-Modern):
    • Modern methods like drip irrigation, sprinklers, and automated systems save more water.
    • Increase crop yield and efficiency.
    • Reduce labor costs and allow irrigation in dry areas.
  4. Rebuttal (Pro-Traditional):
    • Modern systems are costly and not affordable for small farmers.
    • Overuse of technology may harm soil and groundwater.
    • Traditional methods can be combined with modern tweaks for efficiency.
  5. Conclusion (Pro-Traditional):
    • While modern methods improve efficiency, traditional irrigation is sustainable, eco-friendly, and accessible, making it better for long-term farming in many regions.
Q 8

Write a short essay describing what farming might be like when you are 60 years old. You could also draw / paint a picture to illustrate what you envision.

Solution

When I am 60, farming will be highly modern and tech-driven. Fields will be monitored by drones and sensors that check soil, water, and crop health in real-time. Robots and automated machines will plant, water, and harvest crops efficiently, reducing labor. Crops will be grown using precision farming, conserving water and fertilizers, while greenhouses and vertical farms allow year-round production. Farmers will rely on data analytics to predict weather and market demands. Despite technology, farms will remain connected to nature, with sustainable practices protecting soil, water, and biodiversity, ensuring healthy food for everyone.

Q 9

Form small groups and discuss the issues affecting the Ganga basin. Prepare a presentation proposing your solutions and their rationale (your reasons). Share and discuss in class. Your teacher will guide you in this exercise.

Solution

The Ganga is one of India’s most important rivers, supporting millions of people, agriculture, and wildlife. However, it faces serious problems like pollution from industrial waste and sewage, overuse of water, deforestation, and loss of biodiversity. Climate change also affects its flow by melting glaciers. To save the river, we need efficient sewage treatment, strict regulation of industrial waste, afforestation along its banks, and sustainable water use. Raising awareness among people about protecting the Ganga is equally important. With combined efforts, we can restore the river and ensure it continues to support life for future generations.

Q 10

Looking at the crops listed in the section 'Echoes from the Past', which ones do you find in use in your home? What conclusion can you draw from your observations?

Solution

In the section “Echoes from the Past”, crops like rice, wheat, millet, maize, pulses, and oilseeds are mentioned. Observing my home, I notice that rice and wheat are staples used daily in meals, along with pulses like lentils and beans. Occasionally, millet or maize is used in traditional dishes or snacks. These crops have been cultivated for centuries, yet they continue to play an essential role in modern diets. This shows that certain crops are timeless and versatile, able to meet nutritional needs across generations. It also reflects how agriculture has shaped food habits, culture, and economy. While modern farming has introduced new varieties and improved yields, the continued use of these traditional crops highlights their adaptability and importance. From this, we can conclude that ancient agricultural practices still influence our food choices, and preserving such crops ensures both cultural continuity and food security.

sections.label: LET'S EXPLORE
sections.html:
Q 1

Create a list of the foods you typically eat. Identify the grains, pulses, oils, vegetables, and fruits in them. Now locate where you live on a physical map of India. (You may refer to the political map of India at the end of the book if you wish.) Which of the food items do you think grow in and around where you live? What do you think are the reasons that they are cultivated in your region? Which of them comes from other places? Why do you think they are not grown in your area?

Solution

I typically eat rice, wheat chapati, lentils (dal), vegetables like spinach, tomatoes, carrots, and fruits like bananas and apples. I also use cooking oils like mustard and sunflower oil. Rice and wheat are grains, lentils are pulses, vegetables include spinach, tomato, and carrot, and fruits include banana and apple. I live in [your city/state], located in [region, e.g., northern India, near the plains of the Ganga]. Around my area, wheat, rice, lentils, mustard, spinach, and tomatoes are commonly grown because the climate, soil, and water availability suit these crops. Bananas, apples, and some vegetables like carrots are brought from other regions, as they require different climates or altitudes-apples grow better in cooler, hilly regions, while bananas need tropical conditions. They are not grown locally due to unsuitable temperature, rainfall, or soil conditions.

Q 2

Imagine that your state received less than normal monsoon rains for two consecutive years. Which items are likely to disappear from your meals, or are likely to become difficult to access?

Solution

If my state received below-normal monsoon rains for two consecutive years, water-dependent crops like rice, vegetables (tomatoes, spinach), and fruits (bananas) would be severely affected. Rice fields require abundant water, so rice could become scarce or expensive. Vegetables and fruits that need regular irrigation may see lower yields, reducing availability in local markets. Pulses like lentils might also be affected, though they are relatively drought-tolerant. Cooking oils from mustard or sunflower may also see a decline if oilseed production drops. As a result, staple meals like rice with dal and vegetables may become costly or harder to access. People might have to rely on grains and foods that need less water or are imported from other regions. Overall, scarcity would impact both affordability and variety of daily meals.

Q 3

Prepare a list of the crops that are cultivated in your state or union territory in a year and classify them into kharif, rabi, and zaid.

Solution

In my state/union territory, various crops are cultivated throughout the year, classified into three seasons:
Kharif crops (sown with the onset of monsoon, harvested in autumn): Rice, maize, cotton, soybean, and sugarcane. These crops need abundant water and warm temperatures, which coincide with the monsoon season.
Rabi crops (sown in winter, harvested in spring): Wheat, barley, mustard, and gram. These crops require cooler temperatures and rely on residual soil moisture or irrigation for growth.
Zaid crops (grown between rabi and kharif, during summer): Watermelon, cucumber, vegetables, and fodder crops. These crops grow in short-duration and warm-weather conditions.
The classification helps farmers plan sowing and harvesting according to climate, rainfall, and temperature patterns, ensuring optimal yield and efficient use of soil and water resources throughout the year.

Q 4

Try to name at least two states in each of the zones mentioned above.

Solution

Kharif crops:

  • Rice, maize, cotton {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Punjab, Odisha, Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh

Rabi crops:

  • Wheat, barley, mustard {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh

Zaid crops:

  • Watermelon, cucumber, vegetables {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Bihar, Gujarat, West Bengal, Tamil Nadu

These states are chosen based on their climate, soil, and water availability, which suit the respective crop seasons. Kharif crops thrive in monsoon rains, rabi crops need cooler, drier winters, and zaid crops grow in short, warm summer periods.

Q 5

Which type of soil is predominantly found in your state or union territory?

Solution

The predominant soil in my state/union territory is alluvial soil, which is fertile and ideal for growing crops like rice, wheat, sugarcane, and vegetables. It is rich in nutrients and formed by river deposits.

Q 6

Soils tell us stories about places- what might have happened over thousands of years during which the soils were being formed. Take a few samples of soil from your neighbourhood. Look carefully at them. (Examine the soil using a magnifying glass or microscope if you have access to either.) What do the samples consist of? What might be the origins of the soil? Are there signs of human activity that you notice? What might happen to the soil over time on account of this?

Solution

I collected a few soil samples from my neighbourhood to observe their characteristics closely. Using a magnifying glass, I noticed that the soil varies in texture, color, and composition. Some samples were dark brown and rich in organic matter, while others were lighter and sandy. The dark soil likely contains decomposed plant and animal material, indicating that it has been fertile for growing plants over many years. The lighter, sandy soil may have originated from nearby rivers or eroded rocks, carried and deposited over time by wind or water.
On examining the soil, I noticed small stones, tiny roots, and even some earthworms, which indicate healthy soil. Signs of human activity were visible as well-pieces of plastic, bits of paper, and traces of construction dust-showing that human settlements and activities affect the soil composition.
Over time, soil can change due to natural and human factors. Fertile soil may lose nutrients if crops are repeatedly grown without replenishing them, while sandy or eroded soil can become less productive. Human activities like construction, dumping of waste, and excessive use of chemical fertilizers can degrade soil quality, reduce its ability to support plants, and lead to pollution. By understanding the soil’s composition and origin, we can appreciate its role in supporting life and realize the importance of protecting it for future generations.
This study shows that soil is not just dirt-it is a living record of nature’s processes and human influence.

Q 7

Take up a small group project. Identify the types of vegetables, fruits, and trees that grow in the different soil types.

Solution

In our group project, we studied different soil types and the plants they support. Alluvial soil grows rice, wheat, sugarcane, mango, guava, and neem trees. Black soil supports cotton, soybean, sorghum, custard apple, and teak. Red soil is good for groundnut, millet, citrus fruits, and mahua trees. Laterite soil grows tea, coffee, pineapple, and cashew, along with eucalyptus trees. Sandy soil supports watermelon, carrots, and coconut trees. Each soil type’s texture, nutrients, and water-holding capacity determine which crops, fruits, and trees thrive, showing the close connection between soil and agriculture or horticulture.

Q 8

Create a family food flowchart that connects your favourite dishes to the different crops they are prepared from and the corresponding soil types in which they are cultivated.

Solution

A family food flowchart shows how favourite dishes come from crops grown in different soils. For example: Rice pulao {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} rice {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} alluvial soil, Chapati {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} wheat {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} black/alluvial soil, Dal {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} lentils {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} black soil, Vegetable curry {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} vegetables {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} red/alluvial soil, Fruit salad {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} fruits {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} alluvial/laterite soil. It connects meals to crops and soil types, showing how our food depends on agriculture.

Q 9

Identify modern agricultural solutions inspired by traditional practices (for example., neem-based practices).

Solution

Modern agriculture often builds on traditional practices, combining age-old knowledge with technology for efficiency and sustainability. For example, neem-based pest control uses neem extracts to naturally repel insects, reducing the need for chemical pesticides. Similarly, composting and organic manure, a traditional method of enriching soil with plant and animal waste, is now enhanced with biofertilizers and vermicomposting to improve soil fertility and crop yield. Crop rotation, an ancient practice, is applied scientifically to prevent soil depletion and control pests. Mulching, used traditionally to retain soil moisture, is now combined with plastic or organic mulches to reduce evaporation and control weeds. Rainwater harvesting in fields, inspired by traditional water conservation, is integrated with modern irrigation systems like drip or sprinkler irrigation to optimize water use. These approaches show how combining traditional knowledge with modern technology promotes sustainable, productive, and environmentally friendly farming.

Q 10

Listen to the special programmes for farmers on the radio or watch a programme on television for a few days. Write down carefully the information that is shared through these mediums. Make a short report on the categories of information you gathered as well as questions that came to your mind during this activity.

Solution

After listening to special programmes for farmers on the radio and watching agricultural shows on television for several days, I gathered useful information on multiple aspects of farming. The information can be grouped into three main categories:

  1. Crop Management: Guidance on sowing seasons, pest control, use of fertilizers, irrigation techniques, and modern farming methods like drip irrigation.
  2. Market and Economics: Updates on crop prices, government schemes, subsidies, and marketing strategies to help farmers sell their produce profitably.
  3. Weather and Environment: Daily weather forecasts, rainfall predictions, and advice on dealing with climate challenges like drought or floods.

sections.label: The Big Questions
sections.html:
Q 1

What defines a 'neighbour'? Is it just shared land borders?

Solution

The most common definition of neighbours is countries that directly touch each other’s boundaries. For example, India and Nepal are neighbours because they share a land border.
Countries separated by short stretches of sea or ocean are also considered neighbours. For instance, India counts Sri Lanka and the Maldives as maritime neighbours.
Geography scholars note that neighbouring countries are those in close proximity that influence each other through migration, trade, culture, and diplomacy.
So, we can say a “neighbour” in geography is not limited to shared land borders-it also includes countries in close proximity, such as maritime neighbours.

Q 2

How do geography and history influence the nature of India’s relationships with her neighbours?

Solution

Geography shapes India’s relationships through shared borders, rivers, mountains, and seas, affecting trade, security, and cultural exchange. For example, the Himalayas act as a natural barrier with China, while rivers like the Ganga connect India with Bangladesh. History also plays a key role-colonial rule, partition, and past conflicts have influenced trust, alliances, and disputes. Shared cultural and religious ties, migration, and historical trade routes foster cooperation with countries like Nepal and Sri Lanka. Together, geography and history determine friendship, rivalry, and strategic partnerships, shaping India’s diplomatic, economic, and security policies with its neighbours.

Q 3

In what ways are India and her neighbours interconnected today?

Solution

Today, India and her neighbours are closely linked through trade, culture, travel, and cooperation. People cross borders for work, study, and festivals, while countries share rivers, traditions, and history. They also work together on security, climate, and regional peace, showing that neighbours are connected in many ways beyond just borders.

sections.label: LET'S EXPLORE
sections.html:
Q 1

Identify the three large water bodies that surround India in the map.

Solution

India is surrounded by three major water bodies that play an important role in its geography, climate, trade, and cultural contacts with the world. These water bodies are the Arabian Sea, the Bay of Bengal, and the Indian Ocean.
The Arabian Sea lies to the west of India. It borders the western coastal states such as Gujarat, Maharashtra, Goa, Karnataka, and Kerala. This sea has been an important route for trade since ancient times, connecting India with Africa, the Middle East, and Europe. Ports like Mumbai, Kochi, and Kandla developed here, helping India become a major trading nation.
The Bay of Bengal is located to the east of India. It touches the eastern coastal plains of West Bengal, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu. Many large rivers such as the Ganga, Brahmaputra, Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri flow into the Bay of Bengal, making its coastal areas fertile and densely populated. Important ports like Chennai, Visakhapatnam, and Kolkata are situated along this coast.
The Indian Ocean lies to the south of India and gives the country its name. It connects India to Southeast Asia, Africa, and Australia. The Indian Ocean has played a key role in maritime trade, cultural exchange, and strategic importance, making India centrally located in global sea routes.

Q 2

The scene in figure depicts the well-known Hindu myth of samudra manthana or the churning of the ocean. The devas (gods, on the right here) and the asuras (demons, on the left) decided to join forces to churn the cosmic ocean in search of amrita or the nectar of immortality, with the serpent Vāsuki as the churning rope and Vişhnu (above) presiding over the operation - he will eventually deceive the asuras to make sure that the amrita goes to the devas alone. Note also the airport's official name of 'Suvarnabhumi Airport' - does it remind you of something?

A massive sculpture at Bangkok airport

Solution

The scene shown in the figure represents the famous Hindu myth of Samudra Manthana, or the churning of the ocean. According to the legend, the devas and asuras worked together to obtain amrita, the nectar of immortality. Mount Mandara was used as the churning rod, and the serpent Vāsuki served as the rope. Lord Vishnu presided over the process to maintain balance and later ensured that the amrita went only to the devas. During the churning, several valuable objects emerged, symbolizing prosperity and power.
The name “Suvarnabhumi Airport” is significant because Suvarnabhumi means “Land of Gold.” This term appears in ancient Indian texts and refers to Southeast Asian regions known for wealth and trade. The airport’s name reflects the historical and cultural influence of Indian civilization in Southeast Asia, especially through trade, religion, and mythology.

Q 3

Seeing that Tamil is one of Singapore’s official languages, what does this suggest to you about the relationship between south India and Singapore?

A signage in Singapore’s four official languages - English, Mandarin, Tamil and Malay.

Solution

The fact that Tamil is one of Singapore’s official languages suggests a deep and long-standing relationship between South India and Singapore. It indicates that a large number of people from Tamil-speaking regions of South India migrated to Singapore, especially during the colonial period, for trade, labor, and administration. Over time, they became an important part of Singapore’s society, culture, and economy. The recognition of Tamil as an official language shows respect for this community and highlights strong historical, cultural, and economic links between the two regions. It also reflects how Indian culture, traditions, and languages have influenced Southeast Asia through migration and trade, creating lasting connections between South India and Singapore.

sections.label: Questions and activities
sections.html:
Q 1

Explain who a maritime neighbour is with two examples with respect to India.

Solution

Maritime Neighbour is a country that is connected to another country by a shared sea or ocean even without a direct land border.
Two examples with respect to India are Sri Lanka and Thailand.

  • SriLanka is maritime neighbour of India. It is situated towards the southeast of India. SriLanka is divided by a narrow stretch of sea known as the Palk Strait.
    It is only about 32 km away from India making them a close maritime neighbour.
  • Thailand is located to the east of India across the Bay of Bengal and the Andaman Sea. They share a maritime boundary.
    Thailand is connected with India through Trilateral highway which stretches from Manipur through Myanmar to Thailand.
Q 2

How has Buddhism created links with India’s neighbours? Give examples to explain your answer.

Solution

Buddhism has created strong links between India and its neighbours by spreading India’s religious and cultural ideas beyond its borders. Originating in India, Buddhism spread to Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bhutan, Myanmar, Thailand, China, and Tibet through monks, traders, and pilgrims. Sacred sites like Bodh Gaya attract pilgrims from these countries. Indian rulers such as Ashoka sent missionaries abroad, strengthening ties. Buddhist art, architecture, and scriptures in neighbouring countries reflect Indian influence, creating lasting cultural, religious, and historical connections between India and her neighbours.

Q 3

What does ‘open border’ policy mean? How does the India–Nepal ‘open border’ policy affect the lives of people living along the border?

Solution

An open border policy means people of two countries can cross the border freely without passports or visas. The India-Nepal open border allows citizens to live, work, trade, and travel easily in each other’s country. It benefits border communities by improving employment opportunities, trade, cultural exchange, and family ties. People can access markets, education, and healthcare across the border, strengthening social and economic relations.

Q 4

The chapter says, “Being neighbours is not just about geography.” Explain this statement with an example.

Solution

The statement means that neighbourly relationships depend on culture, history, trade, and cooperation, not just shared borders. For example, India and Sri Lanka are separated by the sea, yet they share strong cultural, religious, and trade ties, showing that being neighbours is about connections, not only geography.

Q 5

What are the different ways in which India has helped smaller countries in her neighbourhood? Explain with examples.

Solution

Different ways in which India has helped smaller countries in her neighbourhood.

  1. In Myanmar, India helped to restore the Ananda temple (Bagan) which was damaged by an earthquake and also gifted a 16 feet replica of the Sarnath statue of the Buddha.
  2. In Afghanistan, India supported the development of education healthcare and infrastructure.
    India is also helping Afghanistan in the construction of the Afghan Parliament building and the Zaranj-Delaram Highway which is a great initiative in this direction by India.
  3. In Maldives, India’s immediate help during, 2004 Tsunami, 2014 water crisis in Male, Maldives and COVID-19 pandemic has helped both the countries build a strong bond, socially, culturally, economically and politically.
Q 6

How do shared challenges become opportunities for cooperation? Were there examples in this chapter to illustrate this?

Solution

Shared challenges become opportunities for cooperation when countries work together to solve common problems, benefiting all. For example, in this chapter, India and Bangladesh collaborate on river water management to prevent floods and ensure irrigation. Similarly, India helps neighbours during natural disasters, showing that challenges like floods, climate change, or health crises encourage teamwork and strengthen regional ties.

Q 7

If borders were drawn only by culture and connections, how would the map look different?

Solution

If borders were drawn only by culture and connections, the map would not follow straight political or geographical lines. Regions with shared language, religion, or traditions would form countries, so areas like Bengal, Punjab, or Tamil regions might become single cultural units, even if they cross present-day India, Bangladesh, or Pakistan.

Q 8

On blank maps:
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Label India's neighbours.
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Draw arrows showing cultural flows (e.g., food, festivals, languages) between India and her neighbours.
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Imagine and redraw new "borders of friendship" that connect neighbours through rivers, trade routes, or cultural zones.
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Collect pictures of the flags of the countries listed in this chapter and write your observations.

Solution

sections.label: The Big Questions
sections.html:
Q 1

What major changes characterised this period?

Solution
  1. Political Changes - Regional kingdoms like the Guptas’ successors, Chalukyas, Pallavas, and Pratiharas rose, replacing large empires with smaller, powerful kingdoms.
  2. Economic Changes - Trade expanded within India and with Southeast Asia, West Asia, and China. Urban centres and markets grew.
  3. Social Changes - The caste system became more rigid; guilds and communities influenced social and economic life.
  4. Religious and Cultural Changes - Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism evolved; temple architecture, sculpture, and art flourished.
  5. Technological and Agricultural Changes - Irrigation, metal tools, and new crops improved farming and productivity.

These changes show a shift from large empires to regional kingdoms, with growing trade, cultural expression, and social organization.

Q 2

How did the political, cultural, and religious developments shape India during this period?

Solution

Political, cultural, and religious developments deeply shaped India:

  • Political: The rise of regional kingdoms like the Chalukyas, Pallavas, and Pratiharas brought stability, encouraged local administration, and promoted military and architectural projects.
  • Cultural: Flourishing art, literature, and temple architecture reflected regional identities and royal patronage, preserving local traditions and skills.
  • Religious: Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism influenced daily life, education, and social norms. Temples and monasteries became centres of learning and culture, while pilgrimage routes strengthened connections across regions.

Together, these developments created a vibrant, diverse, and organized society, linking politics, culture, and religion, and laying foundations for India’s social and artistic heritage.

Q 3

What was the impact of foreign invasions and interactions on Indian society and polity during this period?

Solution

Foreign invasions and interactions had notable impacts on Indian society and polity:

  • Political Impact: Invasions by Hunas (Huns) weakened the Gupta Empire, leading to the rise of regional kingdoms and shifts in power.
  • Military Developments: Kingdoms strengthened defenses, forts, and armies to resist invasions.
  • Cultural Impact: Contacts with Central Asia and West Asia introduced new art styles, coins, textiles, and music.
  • Economic Impact: Trade with foreign regions expanded, bringing wealth, luxury goods, and ideas.
  • Social Impact: Migration and settlement of foreigners led to cultural exchanges, new communities, and spread of ideas.

Overall, foreign interactions and invasions reshaped political boundaries, enriched culture, and increased India’s connectivity with the wider world, influencing its social and economic life.

sections.label: LET'S EXPLORE
sections.html:
Q 1

Do you recall where the capital of the earlier Maurya and Gupta empires was located?

Solution
  • The Maurya Empire’s capital was Pataliputra (modern-day Patna, Bihar).
  • The Gupta Empire’s capital was also Pataliputra, which remained an important political and cultural centre during both empires.
Q 2

How do you think this shift may have shaped the sub-continent’s politics in the years to come?

Solution

The shift from large empires like the Mauryas and Guptas to smaller regional kingdoms led to decentralized politics. Local rulers gained more power, which encouraged regional identities, rivalry, and alliances. Over time, this shaped India’s sub-continent into a mosaic of kingdoms, influencing future administration, warfare, trade, and cultural diversity.

Q 3

If you know the Devanagari script, with the help of your teacher try to identify some of the letters in Harṣha’s signature figure.

Solution

Harșha's signature in the image is written in Devanagari script, which was commonly used for Sanskrit and administrative records during his reign. By examining the figure carefully, you can identify letters like "ह" (ha), "ष" (şha), and "र " (ra), which together spell his name "Harşha".
This signature shows how rulers used personal seals or signatures in official documents, combining art and script to assert authority. It also reflects the use of Sanskrit and Devanagari in administration, religion, and culture during the 7th century.

Q 4

Looking at the map can you identify a few mountain passes or desert areas that Xuanzang had to cross while travelling between China and India?

Solution

While travelling between China and India, Xuanzang had to cross several mountain passes and deserts:

  1. Mountain Passes:
    • Himalayas via Karakoram Pass or Hindukush Pass to enter the Indian subcontinent.
    • Kunlun Mountains in western China.
  2. Desert Areas:
    • Taklamakan Desert in present-day Xinjiang.
    • Parts of the Gobi Desert during the northern route in China.

These natural barriers made his journey long, dangerous, and challenging, showing his determination to reach India for studying Buddhism and collecting scriptures.

Q 5

Why was Kannauj so attractive to those powerful dynasties? Observe its location on the map and discuss in class.

Solution

Kannauj was attractive to powerful dynasties because of its strategic location in the Ganga-Yamuna doab, which made it fertile for agriculture and a key trade and political centre. It controlled routes connecting north, central, and eastern India, allowing rulers to dominate commerce, collect taxes, and expand influence. Its wealth and location made it a prized capital for dynasties like the Harṣha, Pratiharas, and Palas.

Q 6

What qualities does Kalhaṇa think a historian should have?

Solution

Kalhaṇa emphasized that a historian should be truthful, wise, and objective, combining careful observation with understanding of society to produce a trustworthy account of the past.

Q 7

What do these lines reveal about his method of gathering information? In what ways does it resemble or differ from the way history is currently written?

Solution

These lines reveal that Kalhaṇa gathered information from multiple sources, such as royal records, inscriptions, local accounts, and oral traditions, and cross-checked them carefully. He aimed to be accurate, critical, and fair in recording events.
This resembles modern history in that today’s historians also use multiple sources, verify facts, and analyze evidence critically. However, it differs because modern historians often rely on archaeology, carbon dating, statistical data, and interdisciplinary research, in addition to written and oral records. Unlike Kalhaṇa, who sometimes included poetic embellishments, contemporary history strives for a more objective and evidence-based approach, minimizing interpretation influenced by literary style.
In essence, Kalhaṇa’s method shows the beginnings of critical historical inquiry, though today’s techniques are more systematic and scientific.

Q 8

Kalhaṇa also writes, "That noble-minded poet is alone worthy of praise whose words, like that of a judge, remain free from love or hatred in relating the facts of the past." What does he mean by 'free from love or hatred'? How would meeting this condition make a historian more reliable?

Solution

By “free from love or hatred,” Kalhaṇa means a historian should be impartial and unbiased, not favouring or opposing any ruler, community, or event due to personal feelings.
Meeting this condition makes a historian more reliable because the account will reflect facts rather than personal opinions, avoiding exaggeration, distortion, or unfair criticism. An impartial historian can present events truthfully and fairly, helping readers trust the accuracy of the history and understand the past without being influenced by the writer’s preferences or grudges.

Q 9

Can you identify some of Durga’s many weapons? And her vahana?

Solution

In the image, Goddess Durga is shown with many weapons in her multiple hands. Some of her weapons include:

  • Trishul (trident)
  • Sword
  • Bow and arrow
  • Mace
  • Conch

Her vahana (mount) is a lion, symbolizing courage, strength, and power. These weapons represent her ability to fight evil and protect righteousness.

Q 10

What are the main differences between her attendants and the demon’s attendants?

Solution

In the image, Durga’s attendants and the demon’s attendants show clear contrasts:

  • Durga’s attendants are calm, composed, and divine, often depicted as animals or gods that support her, like the lion (her vahana), symbolizing courage and righteousness.
  • The demon’s attendants appear chaotic, aggressive, and monstrous, reflecting evil and disorder. They are often ugly or frightening, emphasizing their destructive and negative nature.

The difference highlights the contrast between good and evil, with Durga’s followers representing order, protection, and dharma, while the demon’s followers represent chaos, destruction, and adharma.

Q 11

What does the demon’s slanted posture and general attitude indicate?

Solution

The demon’s slanted posture and aggressive attitude indicate arrogance, hostility, and defiance. His leaning or twisted stance suggests he is unbalanced and chaotic, contrasting with Durga’s calm and controlled posture, symbolizing evil, disorder, and opposition to dharma.

Q 12

What details do you observe in these coins? Why do the coins of Toramāṇa and Skandagupta appear so similar? What conclusions can you draw?

Solution

The coins show portraits of the kings, inscriptions of their names, and symbols of authority, such as weapons or royal emblems. Toramāṇa’s and Skandagupta’s coins appear similar because both rulers adopted comparable designs, styles, and inscriptions to convey legitimacy and continuity.
This suggests that later rulers often imitated earlier ones to gain acceptance among subjects, project authority, and maintain stability. Coin design was a way to show political power, royal identity, and cultural influence, so similarity indicates the influence of established norms and the desire to be seen as rightful and connected to past empires.

Q 13

The Chachnāma records that when King Dāhar was killed, his widowed queen put up a brave resistance, and when the situation became hopeless, she immolated herself along with other ladies. Another queen ordered her treasure to be distributed among the brave soldiers and inspired them to stand against the invaders.

  1. What does this suggest about the way the indigenous people responded to invasions?
  2. Why do you think the queen chose to give away her treasure?
  3. What does this reveal about the role of women in warfare?
Solution
  1. This passage suggests that the indigenous people responded to invasions with courage, loyalty, and resistance, even in desperate situations.
  2. The queen chose to give away her treasure to the soldiers to motivate them, boost morale, and strengthen their resolve to fight against the invaders.
  3. It also reveals that women played active and influential roles in warfare, not just as supporters but as leaders who could inspire bravery, make strategic decisions, and contribute to defending their kingdoms. Their actions were crucial in shaping the course of events during conflicts.

sections.label: THINK ABOUT IT
sections.html:
Q 1

Does the above ceremony remind you of a similar event discussed in Part 1 of this textbook?

Solution

Yes, the ceremony in the image resembles the royal consecration or coronation ceremonies discussed in Part 1 of the textbook, such as the Rajasuya of kings in ancient India, where rituals, gifts, and symbolic gestures established a ruler’s authority and divine approval.

Q 2

Some historians state that Dharmapāla was Buddhist; he certainly patronised Buddhist learning and institutions. But we should keep in mind that in ancient India, the concept of religion was more fluid than it is today, and except perhaps for monks and nuns, people could feel at ease with various belief systems and practise different modes of worship at the same time. Indeed, people often did not regard belief systems involving Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism as separate, but rather as branches of a common philosophical tree. Besides, rulers often took pride in patronising several schools of thought; we saw the case of the Guptas in Part 1 of this textbook, and Harșha gives us one more example of such broad-mindedness. Similarly, several successors of Dharmapāla, though still patronising Buddhist institutions, were said to be devotees of Śhiva. The Rāșhțrakūṭas and other rulers will provide more examples of this.

Solution

During ancient India, religion was practiced in a more flexible and inclusive way than it often is today. Dharmapāla, the ruler of the Pāla dynasty, is considered a Buddhist because he strongly patronised Buddhist monasteries, universities, and learning centres. However, it is important to note that people-and even kings-were generally comfortable with multiple belief systems. Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism were often seen not as completely separate religions but as different branches of a shared philosophical tradition, allowing coexistence and mutual respect.
Rulers like Dharmapāla often supported various religious schools simultaneously. For instance, while he promoted Buddhism, many of his successors were devotees of Śiva, a Hindu deity, yet continued to support Buddhist institutions. This was not unusual; earlier, the Gupta rulers also patronised multiple faiths, and later rulers like Harṣha did the same. Such broad-mindedness encouraged religious harmony, intellectual exchange, and the flourishing of art, literature, and learning. It also allowed monasteries, temples, and educational centres to become hubs where scholars of different traditions could interact, debate, and innovate.
In summary, ancient Indian rulers, including Dharmapāla, contributed to a pluralistic and tolerant society, where philosophical, cultural, and religious diversity was valued and actively promoted. This approach helped shape India’s rich spiritual and cultural heritage.

Q 3

Sulaiman, a 9th-century Arab merchant and traveller, makes this comment on a Gurjara-Pratīhāra ruler: "This king maintains numerous forces, and no other Indian prince has so fine a cavalry. He is unfriendly to the Arabs ... there is no greater foe of the Muhammadan faith than he.... He has riches, and his camels and horses are numerous. ... There is no country in India safer from robbers." Why do you think the author calls the ruler a 'foe to the Muslim faith'?

Solution

The author calls the Gurjara-Pratīhāra ruler a “foe to the Muslim faith” because the king resisted Arab invasions and defended his kingdom against Muslim armies. His strong military, especially cavalry, and wealth made him a powerful opponent, preventing the spread of Arab influence in his territory. The statement reflects the political and military conflicts of the time, not necessarily religious hostility, showing how rulers defended their lands and sovereignty against foreign powers.

sections.label: Questions and activities
sections.html:
Q 1

If you lived in Kannauj during the struggle between the Palas, the Pratiharas and the Rashtrakuṭas, how would it change your daily life and your estimate of the rulers? Write a letter on this to your friend living in Kanchipuram.

Solution

Dear Friend,
I hope you are well in Kanchipuram. Life here in Kannauj has changed a lot with the struggle between the Pālas, Pratīhāras, and Rāṣhṭrakūṭas. Soldiers are everywhere, and markets are sometimes disrupted because of the conflicts. People are anxious, and trade has slowed down, making daily life uncertain.
Despite the chaos, I am amazed by the rulers’ strength and strategy. Their armies, forts, and clever alliances show their power, but their constant fighting makes life difficult for ordinary citizens like us. I have learned that kings may be brave and skilled, yet their ambitions affect the people most.
I hope peace will return soon so that trade, learning, and festivals can continue without fear. I look forward to hearing about life in Kanchipuram.
Your friend,
[Your Name]

Q 2

Emperors and kings during this period controlled only core regions and governed other regions through subordinate vassals. What would be the advantages and the challenges of such a system?

Solution

Advantages:

  • Emperors could focus on core regions and ensure strong administration there.
  • Using vassals allowed control over distant areas without direct governance.
  • Vassals managed local affairs, collected taxes, and maintained armies, reducing the ruler’s burden.

Challenges:

  • Vassals could become too powerful and challenge the emperor’s authority.
  • Loyalty was not always guaranteed, leading to rebellions.
  • Communication and coordination with distant regions were difficult, affecting law, trade, and security.

This system gave rulers flexibility and reach, but also risked instability if vassals acted independently or rebelled.

Q 3

In what ways were the invasions of the Hunas and the Arabs different in their aims, methods, and impact on the Indian subcontinent? Prepare a write-up, discuss and present it in the class.

Solution

The Hunas invaded India in the 5th–6th centuries. Their aim was mostly plunder and territorial expansion. They attacked the Gupta Empire, using brutal warfare and cavalry raids. Their invasions caused political instability, weakened the empire, and disrupted trade and agriculture. However, they were eventually absorbed into Indian society, leaving some cultural influences.
The Arab invasions in the 7th-8th centuries, especially in Sindh, had different aims: spreading Islam, controlling trade routes, and establishing administrative footholds. The Arabs used well-organized armies and naval support. Their impact included the introduction of Islamic culture, religion, and administration in western India. Unlike the Hunas, Arab rule led to long-lasting cultural and religious connections.
In summary, while the Hunas’ invasions were mainly destructive and temporary, the Arab invasions had religious, cultural, and political consequences that shaped parts of the subcontinent for centuries.

Q 4

Imagine you are a common citizen watching the Prayaga assembly. How would you react to Harṣha giving away most of his wealth?

Solution

As a common citizen watching the Prayaga assembly, I would feel amazed and inspired seeing Harṣha give away most of his wealth. It would show me that he is generous, just, and devoted to dharma, valuing the welfare of people over personal riches. I would likely feel proud to live under such a king and motivated to follow his example of charity and fairness in daily life.

Q 5

Forming groups, let each group choose one Alvar and one Nayanar and prepare a biography poster or booklet. Include their life stories and a sample poem or two (in translation).

Solution

For your group project, you can focus on one Alvar, such as Nammalvar, and one Nayanar, like Appar (Tirunavukkarasar). Your biography should describe their life stories, including their birthplace, family background, early experiences, and spiritual journey. Highlight their devotion-Nammalvar’s love for Vishnu and Appar’s devotion to Shiva-and their contributions through hymns, poems, and temple worship. Include one or two sample poems in English translation, showing Nammalvar’s praise of Vishnu’s qualities and Appar’s expression of surrender and moral guidance. Add illustrations of temples, portraits, or instruments, and design the poster or booklet with South Indian motifs to make it visually appealing. The final presentation should convey their devotion, literary contributions, and lasting impact on the Bhakti movement.

Q 6

You will notice that our maps show only ancient names for the kingdoms’ capitals and main cities. Using a pencil, mark the modern names next to their original names. Refer the current map of India and try to locate those cities on it.

Solution

To complete this activity, take your ancient map of India and a modern map side by side. For each ancient capital or city, mark its modern name with a pencil next to the original:

  • Pataliputra {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Patna
  • Ujjayini {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ujjain
  • Kanchipuram {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Kanchipuram (same name)
  • Kannauj {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Kannauj (same name)
  • Mathura {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mathura (same name)
  • Tāmralipti {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Tamluk

After marking the names, locate each city on the current map of India to understand how the ancient kingdoms correspond to modern states and regions. This will help you connect historical locations with present-day geography.

sections.label: The Big Questions
sections.html:
Q 1

Why are the 11th and 12th centuries seen as a period of transition in Indian history?

Solution

The 11th and 12th centuries are seen as a period of transition because large regional kingdoms replaced earlier empires, changing political structures. There were shifts in trade, culture, and religion, with new centers of power emerging. Frequent invasions and internal conflicts also reshaped society, laying the foundation for the medieval period of Indian history.

Q 2

Which new powers emerged during this period? What were the essential features of their economic, military and administrative systems?

Solution

During the 11th-12th centuries, new powers like the Cholas in the south, the Gahadavalas in the north, and the Sena dynasty in Bengal emerged.
Economic systems: They relied on agriculture, trade, and taxation, with thriving urban markets and overseas trade, especially the Cholas with Southeast Asia.
Military systems: Armies included infantry, cavalry, and war elephants, with strong naval forces for the Cholas. Forts and strategic defense were important.
Administrative systems: Kings governed core regions directly and managed distant territories through vassals or local chiefs. Land grants to temples and officials were common, supporting administration, religion, and culture.
These features show a mix of centralized control in core areas and decentralized management in peripheral regions, adapting to political and economic conditions of the time.

Q 3

What high accomplishments in art, architecture, literature, science, etc., do we come across during this period?

Solution

During the 11th and 12th centuries, India witnessed remarkable achievements across art, architecture, literature, and science.
In architecture, the Chola temples like Brihadeeswarar Temple displayed intricate stone carvings, towering vimanas, and advanced engineering. In the north, temples of Khajuraho and Jain monuments in Gujarat showcased detailed sculpture and ornamentation.
Literature flourished in Sanskrit, Tamil, and regional languages, with works on poetry, philosophy, law, and devotion by poets, scholars, and saints.
In art, bronze sculptures, temple murals, and carvings reached high levels of skill and realism.
Science and technology progressed in astronomy, mathematics, and metallurgy, with precise astronomical calculations and durable metal structures.
These accomplishments reflect a blend of religious devotion, royal patronage, and regional creativity, leaving a lasting legacy in India’s cultural and intellectual history.

sections.label: LET'S EXPLORE
sections.html:
Q 1

Observe the sketch of the Khyber Pass. What kind of terrain does it depict?
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} What advantages would it offer to an army trying to reach the Indus plains? But also, what dangers?
{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Thinking of the caravans of traders that passed through the Khyber Pass for many centuries, let's ask the same questions about the advantages as well as dangers.

Solution

The sketch of the Khyber Pass depicts a rugged, narrow, mountainous terrain with steep slopes and rocky paths. It is a natural mountain corridor cutting through the hills between Central Asia and the Indian subcontinent.
For an army, the Khyber Pass offered the advantage of being one of the few accessible routes into the Indus plains, making it strategically important for invasions. Its narrow paths allowed a disciplined army to advance toward fertile regions. However, it was also dangerous because armies could face ambushes from higher ground, landslides, harsh weather, and difficulties in moving large forces, supplies, and animals.
For trading caravans, the pass provided a crucial trade route connecting India with Central Asia, West Asia, and beyond. It enabled the exchange of goods, ideas, and culture. At the same time, traders faced dangers such as robbers, difficult terrain, extreme weather, and lack of food or shelter. Thus, while the Khyber Pass was vital for movement and contact, it was always challenging and risky for both soldiers and traders.

Q 2

In later centuries, the Somanātha temple was rebuilt and destroyed again several times; the existing temple was built in 1950 and inaugurated the next year by the then President of India, Rajendra Prasad. Why do you think it was decided to fund the construction entirely from public donations?

Solution

It was decided to fund the rebuilding of the Somanatha temple entirely through public donations to show that the temple belonged to the people of India, not the government. This respected India’s secular principles, avoided state involvement in religious construction, and symbolized collective faith, unity, and resilience after centuries of destruction and rebuilding.

Q 3

In the painting, can you make out who is the king and who is the guru? What does the king’s posture and general attitude express?

Solution

In the painting, the king can be identified as the figure seated respectfully with folded hands, while the guru is shown in a calm, composed posture, often seated slightly higher or in a position of authority. The king’s bowed head and humble posture express reverence, devotion, and respect toward the guru. This attitude shows that even powerful rulers valued spiritual guidance and learning, acknowledging the guru’s moral and intellectual authority over royal power.

Q 4

Writing some 60 years after Bakhtiyār Khiljīs campaign through Bihar, the historian Minhaj al-Siraj Jūzjānī, recorded in his Tabakāt-i-Nāsirī how Bakhtiyar's forces captured a 'fortress' and "acquired great booty." He adds, "The greater number of the inhabitants of that place were Brahmans, and all those Brahmans had their heads shaven; and they were all slain. There were a great number of books there. ... It was found that the whole of that fortress and city was a college, and in [their] tongue, they call a college bihär."
Remembering the meaning of vihāra and noting that Nālandā was known in ancient inscriptions as 'Nālandā Mahāvihāra', can you find two more clues in the above passage to identify the 'fortress' near the Nālandā university? (Hint: Who do you think these 'Brahmans' actually were?)

Solution

The passage gives two clear clues that the “fortress” was actually Nālandā Mahāvihāra.
First, it says that the inhabitants were called “Brahmans” with shaved heads. These were not Hindu Brahmins but Buddhist monks, who traditionally shaved their heads and lived in monasteries.
Second, it mentions a large number of books and that the entire place was a college, called bihār in their language. The word vihāra means a Buddhist monastery or learning centre, and Nālandā was famously known as a great centre of Buddhist learning with vast libraries.
Together, these clues clearly identify the “fortress” as Nālandā University, showing how a renowned educational institution was mistaken for a military fort.

Q 5

In Kalyăṇī, Basavaṇṇa established an anubhava maṇḍapa (literally, 'pavilion of experience'), where men and women from every social, economic, religious or linguistic background, including saints and philosophers, could assemble to discuss all aspects of life, including moral values and religion.

  1. Why, in your opinion, did Basava want people from all backgrounds to come together and exchange ideas?
  2. If something like an anubhava mandapa existed today, what important topics do you think people ought to discuss there?
  3. What lessons can we draw from the anubhava mandapa spirit to create a just and civil society today?
Solution
  1. Basava wanted people from all backgrounds to come together because he believed that true knowledge and moral understanding grow through open dialogue and shared experience, not through caste, gender, or social barriers. By bringing everyone together, he challenged inequality and promoted equality, rational thinking, and social reform, especially against caste discrimination and rigid religious practices.
  2. If an anubhava maṇḍapa existed today, people should discuss issues such as social equality, gender justice, environmental protection, religious harmony, education, poverty, and ethical use of technology. These are concerns that affect everyone and need collective thinking.
  3.  The spirit of the anubhava maṇḍapa teaches us the importance of dialogue, inclusiveness, and respect for diverse opinions. It shows that a just and civil society can be built when people listen to one another, question injustice, and work together for the common good, beyond differences of caste, class, or belief.

sections.label: THINK ABOUT IT
sections.html:
Q 1

We will meet later in this chapter the Persian scholar al-Bīrūnī, who accompanied Mahmūd in some of his campaigns. He wrote in his memoirs on India, "The Hindu Shāhiya dynasty is now extinct, and of the whole house there is no longer the slightest remnant in existence. We must say that, in all their grandeur, they never slackened in the ardent desire of doing that which is good and right, that they were men of noble sentiment and noble bearing."
What conclusions can we draw from this remark by someone in Mahmud's entourage?

Solution

This remark shows that al-Biruni was objective and fair, even while being part of Mahmud’s entourage. He acknowledges that although the Hindu Shahiya dynasty was defeated and became extinct, its rulers were upright, noble, and committed to good governance. This suggests that enemies could still respect Indian rulers for their moral values and conduct. It also shows that historical accounts were not always biased and that some foreign scholars tried to judge Indian dynasties on merit, not merely from the victor’s perspective.

sections.label: Questions and activities
sections.html:
Q 1

Why is the period under consideration in this chapter regarded as a major transition in Indian history? Give two examples each of change and continuity from this period.

Solution

The period discussed in this chapter is regarded as a major transition because India moved from early medieval regional kingdoms to new political, social, and cultural patterns, while still retaining many older traditions.
Examples of change:
First, political power shifted from large empires to many regional kingdoms, such as the Pālas, Pratīhāras, Cholas, and Rāṣhṭrakūṭas. Second, there was an increase in foreign interactions and invasions, such as those by the Arabs and later Turkic rulers, which reshaped politics and society.
Examples of continuity:
First, religious traditions continued, with Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism still shaping social and cultural life, supported by rulers. Second, trade, temple-building, and learning centres like monasteries and temples remained important, showing continuity in economic and cultural practices.
Thus, the period shows both significant transformation and enduring traditions, marking it as a transitional phase in Indian history.

Q 2

Observe figure in the previous chapter and draw a similar ‘star of dynasties’ gathering all or most of the dynasties that appear in this chapter.

Solution

To draw a ‘star of dynasties’ like the one in the previous chapter, follow this method:
Draw a large star or circle in the centre and write “Early Medieval India (6th-12th centuries)” inside it. From the centre, draw spokes or arms outward. At the end of each arm, write the name of a major dynasty from this chapter.
Include dynasties such as the Harṣhavardhana, Pālas, Gurjara-Pratīhāras, Rāṣhṭrakūṭas, Chalukyas, Pallavas, Cholas, Senas, Gahadavalas, and Hindu Shāhiyas. You may group northern, southern, and eastern dynasties on different sides of the star for clarity.
Optionally, add small notes near each name (region or capital) using light pencil. This visual shows how many dynasties coexisted and competed, helping you understand the political diversity of the period.

Q 3

Taking a map of the Subcontinent, draw a geographical tour that covers all or most dynasties in this chapter (you may draw inspiration from some of the ‘travels’ the chapter’s narration follows).

Solution

Q 4

With the help of a map of India and Southeast Asia, can you calculate the approximate distance that Rājendra I’s fleet of ships had to navigate to reach their objective?

Solution

Q 5

Match these two pairs:

Column - A Column - B
(a) Eastern Gangas (i) Belur
(b) Chandellas (ii) Bṛihadishvara temple
(c) Paramaras (iii) Konark Sun temple
(d) Hoysalas (iv) Kandariya Mahadeva temple
(e) Cholas (v) Bhojeshwar temple
Solution
  • (a) Eastern Gangas {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (iii) Konark Sun Temple
  • (b) Chandellas {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (iv) Kandariya Mahadeva Temple
  • (c) Paramaras {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (v) Bhojeshwar Temple
  • (d) Hoysalas {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (i) Belur
  • (e) Cholas {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (ii) Bṛihadishvara Temple
Q 6

Working in groups, compare the dynasties in this chapter and in the preceding one; create a table to list the dynasties present in both, those that disappear from the preceding period, and those that appear in this chapter’s period.

Solution
Category Dynasties
Dynasties present in both periods Guptas (late phase), Vakaṭakas
Dynasties that disappear from the preceding period Mauryas, Śuṅgas, Kuṣhaṇas, Satavahanas
Dynasties that appear in this chapter’s period Harṣhavardhana, Palas, Gurjara-Pratiharas, Raṣhṭrakuṭas, Chalukyas, Pallavas, Cholas, Hoysalas, Eastern Gangas, Chandellas, Paramaras, Senas, Gahadavalas, Hindu Shahiyas
Q 7

Using the chapter and any additional reading, prepare a short note explaining (1) why centres of learning like Nalanda were important; (2) how their destruction may have affected education and culture in India.

Solution

Centers of Learning like Nalanda
